sections.heading: 2. Fundamental Courses
sections.rule_text: Students complete two courses by either taking two courses from group A or one course from group A and one course from group B. (At least one course must be taken from group A). Students who have taken equivalent courses in the past and received grades of at least a B may apply for waivers and take other CS courses instead.

Causal Inference (Previously listed as COMS 4995: Causal Inference)

sections.heading: 4. General Electives
sections.rule_text: Students must complete the remaining credits of General Elective Courses at the 4000 level or above. At least three of these points must be chosen from either the Electives listed above or from the CS department at the 4000 level or higher. Students may also request to use at most 3 points of Non-CS/Non-pathway coursework if approved by the process listed below.

sections.heading: 1. Breadth Courses (12 credits)
sections.rule_text: Visit the breadth requirement page for more information.

sections.heading: 2. Courses (18 credits)
sections.rule_text: Students will build their course plan with their Faculty Advisor to ensure that their courses meet the requirements of the MS 30-credit program. Students must take at least 6 credits of 6000-level graduate courses selected from Computer Science and/or related areas together with your faculty advisor. At most, 3 credits overall may be from “non-COMS” graduate courses. All MS students can take a maximum of 12 project/research points(CS and non-CS). No more than 12 credits of project/research points can count towards the 30-credit requirements. Please visit the Directory of Classes to get the updated course listings. Please also note that not all courses are offered every semester or even every year. A few courses are offered only once every two or three years or even less frequently. Please note that some Data Science Institute courses, such as COMS/CSEE W4121 (Computer Systems for Data Science), do not count towards the CS MS degree. If you have any questions, please contact your advisor or CS Advising . Find open faculty positions here .

sections.heading: 1. BREADTH REQUIREMENT
sections.rule_text: Visit the breadth requirement page for more information.

sections.heading: 2. FUNDAMENTAL COURSES (9 credits)
sections.rule_text: Students must take 9 credits of COMS E6902 Thesis. The points are typically spread over multiple semesters, e.g., 3 points each for 3 semesters or 4.5 points each for 2 semesters. No more than 9 points of E6902 may be taken. Sign up for the section number of E6902 associated with your thesis advisor.

sections.heading: 3. SECONDARY COURSES
sections.rule_text: Students are required to complete 9 elective credits of graduate courses (4000-level or above) selected from Computer Science and/or related areas, together with their faculty thesis advisor. These would normally be strongly related to your thesis topic. Up to 3 of these points may be in COMS E6901 Projects in Computer Science.

sections.heading: 4. GENERAL ELECTIVES
sections.rule_text: Students must complete the remaining credits of General Elective Courses at the 4000 level or above. At least three of these points must be chosen from the CS department at the 4000 level or higher. Students may also request to use at most 3 points of Non-CS/Non-pathway coursework if approved by the process listed below. All MS students can take a maximum of 12 project/research points(CS and non-CS), 9 of which can be thesis points if you are in the MS Thesis pathway. No more than 12 credits of project/research points can count towards the 30-credit.

sections.heading: 5. THESIS DEFENSE
sections.rule_text: A thesis proposal is presented to your thesis committee at least three months before your defense. Your thesis committee should have three members. Two of them must be internal, but one can be an outsider. Please bring the thesis defense form to your defense. Once completed, please submit the form to CS Advising via email: ms-advising@cs.columbia.edu. The thesis cannot be imported from another institution. A publication-quality thesis document is also published as a CS department technical report. Once completed, please upload your thesis into MICE.
